clinical_coherence.notes: 22F Hispanic with GAD presenting with predominantly psychiatric symptoms (paranoia, hallucinations) but crucially with normal CSF (3 WBC, normal protein, normal glucose) and subtle exam findings (brief staring spells as possible subclinical seizures). The normal CSF creates genuine diagnostic difficulty. The preceding viral prodrome and family autoimmune history (mother with SLE) provide subtle clues. This is a well-constructed puzzle where the CSF does not help.

tool_output_consistency.notes: CSF is remarkably normal (3 WBC, protein 37.6, glucose 58.1) with NMDAR antibodies sent but pending. EEG shows extreme delta brush -- the key diagnostic finding. MRI shows subtle bilateral temporal FLAIR changes. The contrast between normal CSF and abnormal EEG creates the puzzle.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD G04.81 correct. Critical actions correctly emphasize sending NMDAR antibodies even when CSF appears normal, recognizing subtle clinical red flags, and not accepting a primary psychiatric diagnosis without complete workup. The teaching that NMDAR encephalitis can have normal routine CSF parameters is a critical pearl.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Outstanding calibration as a diagnostic puzzle. Normal CSF, predominant psychiatric presentation, pre-existing GAD, and pending antibodies all create genuine uncertainty. The subtle clinical clues (staring spells, viral prodrome, autoimmune family history) require careful clinical acumen.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 22F Hispanic with GAD and family history of SLE is realistic and appropriate for NMDAR encephalitis.

educational_value.notes: Outstanding teaching case: NMDAR encephalitis with normal CSF, the importance of sending antibodies regardless of routine CSF results, the danger of premature psychiatric diagnosis, and the EEG as the key to unlocking the diagnosis. This teaches a critical clinical pitfall that is commonly encountered.

strengths: Normal CSF creating genuine diagnostic difficulty is an outstanding teaching point
EEG with extreme delta brush as the key diagnostic clue when other tests are unrevealing
Subtle clinical red flags (staring spells, viral prodrome) requiring careful observation
Teaches that NMDAR antibodies should be sent regardless of routine CSF results
The pre-existing GAD creating anchoring bias is a realistic cognitive trap
